data.title: Kenvue Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Kenvue Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Clear Path To Double Digits Return - Tariffs Pose Minimal Headwinds
data.description:
ET Is An Even Better Buy After The Steep Correction, As Power Generation Demand Remains Robust & Tariffs Impose Minimal Impacts

We previously covered Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) in March 2025, discussing why the ongoing correction had triggered the richer forward distribution yields, driven by the robust FY2024 adj EBITDA growth and the rich FY2025 guidance.

Combined with the aggressive capex investments in pipelines, processing plants, and data centers, with it well supporting its double digits upside potential, we had reiterated our Buy rating upon a further pullback to the prior support ranges of $16.50 and $15.20.

ET 1Y Stock Price

Since then, the worst of the tariff induced meltdown has already triggered the drastic retracement to our recommended Buy point of $15 ranges, as the bulls also swopped in to defend ET's 2024 support levels, aided by the recent 90 days tariff pause announced on April 09, 2025.

While OPEC+ may have accelerated its output hikes while triggering the moderation in crude oil/natural gas spot prices nearer to pre-pandemic levels, we are not overly concerned indeed, since the industry has been one that is typically cyclical while dependent on the macro events.

If anything, readers must note ET's excellent growth capex efforts, with it already triggering its volume growth on a YoY basis and growing adj EBITDA to $4.1B in FQ1'25 (+5.6% YoY).

This implies that the management's reiterated FY2025 adj EBITDA guidance of $16.3B at the midpoint (+5.2% YoY) does not appear to be overly ambitious, especially since 90% of its bottom-line prospects are tied to fee-based pricing "with limited commodity price exposure."

This is significantly aided by its "high percentage of take or pay contracts helping to reduce impacts from market volatility," while building upon the robust adj EBITDA growth observed in FY2024 to $15.48B (+13% YoY):

Take-or-Pay Contract are written agreements between a buyer and seller that obligate the buyer to pay regardless of whether or not the seller delivers the good or service...

The clause of the Take-or-Pay Contract which stipulates this obligation for the buyer to pay a compensation in the case he does not take the good or service is commonly called the killing clause. (2b1stconsulting)

The same may be observed in ET's Distributable Cash Flow attributable to partners at $2.3B in FQ1'25 (-2.1% YoY) and $8.36B in FY2024 (+10.4% YoY), allowing them to sustain the rich distribution pay-outs ahead.

These expanded bottom-lines already trigger the healthier balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 3.65x as well, compared to FY2024 levels of 3.85x and FY2019 levels of 4.90x, significantly aided by its growing partnerships and the well diversified capital funding.

Moving forward, we believe that ET's long-term prospects remain robust, especially given the durability of AI demand and the ongoing data center capex boom, as similarly reiterated by numerous market leaders/hyperscalers, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT) in the recent earning calls.

The same promising commentaries have been offered by Equinix, Inc. (EQIX), the global retail colocation leader with 21% in 2024 market share, as they also raise their FY2025 revenue and AFFO per share guidance, as Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR) also reports growing data center backlog through 2027.

These developments further underscore why the data center related demand for ET's power generation capabilities are likely to remain robust, as similarly reported by the management in the recent earnings call, with many of its prior discussions for new power plants, data centers, and industrial and onshore manufacturing capacities already reaching advanced levels.

This is significantly aided by its "solid backlog of well contracted growth projects with strong counterparties" over the next five years and the numerous natural gas fired electric generation facilities/pipelines/processing plants expected to commence service in 2025/2026.

These developments are likely to trigger ET's robust prospects no matter the uncertain macroeconomic environment and the volatile commodity spot prices, as the management also rapidly monetizes its intensified capex efforts.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These excellent numbers may have directly contributed to the consensus' raised forward estimates, with the ET expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +5.5%/+4.4% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +4.2%/+4.1%, albeit decelerating compared to the 5Y historical growth at a CAGR of +8.8%/+6.7% and 10Y historical growth at +4%/+12.2%, respectively.

ET Valuations

Despite the potentially painful oil/gas industry's normalization period ahead, we believe that ET is likely to remain highly profitable, as guided by the management.

At the same time, the management has guided minimal tariff impacts on its ongoing constructions and future gas imports from China, as the latter country also waives off their "125% tariff on ethane imports from the United States imposed earlier this month."

These reasons are also why ET remains reasonably valued at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 8.03x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 13.03x, nearer to its 1Y mean of 8.29x/13.58x and its 5Y mean of 7.78x/9.11x, respectively, after the much-needed correction from 2025 heights of 9.18x/16.07x.

The pullback has also triggered its compelling valuations compared to its direct pipeline peers, including Dominion Energy (D) at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 12.09x, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) at 9.53x, Plains All American (PAA) at 8.58x, MPLX LP (MPLX) at 10.13x, and Kinder Morgan (KMI) at 11.29x, respectively, with it offering interested investors with an excellent margin of safety.

So, Is ET Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ET 5Y Stock Price

For now, ET has already lost -26.2% or the equivalent -$17.93B of its market capitalization from the January 2025 peaks, as the stock also appears to be well-supported at its recent floor of $15s ranges.

Based on the LTM net income per common unit of $1.32 (+21.1% sequentially) and the 1Y P/E non-GAAP mean of 13.03x, the stock is finally trading below our updated fair value estimates of $17.20.

Despite the consensus lowered FY2027 net income per common unit estimates from $1.80 to $1.64, there remains an excellent upside potential of +34.7% to our long-term price target of $21.30 as well, thanks to the deep sell-off.

Combined with ET's richer forward distribution yields of 8.09%, compared to its 4Y historical averages of 7.42% and the sector median of 5.19%, the moderating short interest volumes by -41% from recent heights, and the bullish support at the recent floor of $15s ranges, we are reiterating our Buy rating here.

data.title: Invesco EQV European Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance quoted is past performance and cannot guarantee comparable future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Visit invesco.com for the most recent month-end performance. Performance figures reflect reinvested distributions and changes in net asset value (NAV). Investment return and principal value will vary so that you may have a gain or a loss when you sell shares. Returns less than one year are cumulative; all others are annualized. Performance shown prior to the inception date of Class R6 shares is that of Class A shares and includes the 12b-1 fees applicable to Class A shares. Index source: RIMES Technologies Corp. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed in the past, returns would have been lower. Performance shown at NAV does not include the applicable front-end sales charge, which would have reduced the performance.

Class Y and R6 shares have no sales charge; therefore performance is at NAV. Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. Class R6 shares are closed to most investors. Please see the prospectus for more details.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • The first quarter of 2025 was marked by volatility and shifts in market leadership. Developed markets in Europe outperformed US equities. German equities had robust gains, driven by increased deficit spending aimed at supporting economic growth and defense. Despite ongoing political issues, French equities also posted positive double-digit returns in US dollar terms. UK equities posted positive returns as well, aided by the fact that many UK companies reported robust earnings, boosting investor confidence. Conversely, US equities declined, affected by apparent uncertainty about US trade policy and the rise of Chinese artificial intelligence company DeepSeek, which put downward pressure on US technology and consumer discretionary sectors. In this market environment, commodities, especially gold, had robust returns. Value stocks outperformed, while small-cap stocks lagged.
  • Our team remains focused on applying our long-term, bottom-up EQV investment process that seeks to identify attractively valued, high quality growth companies. We expect geopolitical and macro concerns to remain elevated, which has historically favored our traditional fundamental approach. We remain optimistic that our conservative quality growth style is well-positioned for the current environment.

As of 03/31/25. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.

During the quarter, we initiated positions in the following stocks:

AstraZeneca is a UK-based multinational pharmaceutical and biotech company focused on discovery and development of innovative medicines in areas including cardiovascular, oncology and respiratory. The company faced temporary setbacks due to employee investigations in China and issues with its oncology drug. Despite these challenges, we believe AstraZeneca's strong product pipeline and undervalued shares present a promising investment opportunity.

Barclays is a UK-based multinational bank with retail and commercial banking in the UK, a global investment bank and a specialist US consumer bank. We have seen improving visibility for earnings and quality after a challenging period. The company has been taking action to simplify the organization, cut costs, improve accountability and enhance capital allocation. We believe Barclays is attractively valued.

BNP Paribas is a France-based multinational bank with operations in retail and investment banking, asset management and insurance. Due to its lower sensitivity to rising interest rates and French political pressures, BNP has underperformed in a strong sector. However, we see it as an interesting prospect if rates stay higher for longer and European economic activity picks up. We believe BNP is attractively valued, with an outlook for improving returns.

Legrand is a France-based global specialist in electrical and digital building infrastructures. The company has underperformed due to weak European construction markets and France’s political uncertainty. However, we believe that will change, and we expect good results from Legrand’s faster-growing segments and a cyclical recovery in core infrastructure products.

We sold the following positions:

Coloplast is a Danish health care company that we sold due to deteriorating earnings and quality trends. The stock’s valuation has continued to trade at a premium relative to its sector, so we sold it to make way for better EQV ideas.

Rentokil Initial is a British pest-control company that we exited due to questions about cost and timing for a turnaround of poor performance in its North American business.

Aalberts is a Dutch manufacturing company that has done an admirable job restructuring its internal manufacturing base and cost optimization. However, without the catalyst of this self-help going forward, we decided to sell in favor of other industrial ideas that, in our view, offer better growth profiles and valuation.

The fund’s consumer discretionary and consumer staples holdings outperformed those of the benchmark sectors and were the largest contributors to relative performance. Geographically, stock selection in Germany, Switzerland and Sweden were notable contributors to relative return.

Conversely, the largest detractors from relative return were stock selection in health care, industrials, financials and communication services. An underweight in financials hampered relative return as well. Geographically, fund holdings in France and the UK underperformed those of the benchmark index and were among the largest detractors from relative return. Exposure in

Israel, a country not represented in the benchmark, also detracted from relative results.

Contributors to performance

Below are the largest contributors to absolute return for the quarter:

Deutsche Boerse is a German stock exchange operator that benefited from increased trading volumes and strong financial results.

flatexDEGIRO is a leading pan-European discount broker. The stock performed well due to customer growth, increased assets under custody, and higher transaction volumes.

BAE Systems is a UK-based aerospace, defense and information security company that has benefited from rising global defense spending, given increasing NATO budgets. The company’s record backlog provides in our view good visibility for earnings and cash flow growth.

Detractors from performance

Below are the largest detractors from absolute return for the quarter:

Teva Pharmaceutical is a leading generics and pharmaceuticals company. The stock declined after a disappointing earnings update regarding profitability, which was affected by higher costs. Our thesis of improving earnings and quality as Teva’s management focuses on building out its innovative pharma portfolio remains intact. Valuation relative to earnings potential remains attractive, in our view.

Novo Nordisk is a Danish pharmaceuticals company. The stock fell after a couple of rare disappointments in its product development pipeline and weaker-than-expected prescription trends for its leading obesity drug, which has been under supply constraints. We trimmed the fund’s position to manage exposure.

ICON is a multinational clinical research organization. Weaker spending by large pharmaceutical firms and a challenging outlook for biotech funding have affected ICON’s short-term growth and earnings prospects, negatively affecting the stock. While demand has remained a bit challenged, the recent earnings update showed some signs of improvement in ICON’s environment, while its lower valuation appears to reflect a lot of the transitory headwinds that Icon and its industry have been facing.

Invesco EQV European Equity Fund Q1 2025 by Invesco US

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Market Is Wrong About Omega Healthcare Investors
data.description:
I have written about Omega Healthcare Investors (NYSE:OHI) many times and have given multiple buy recommendations. So far, the stock price has been moving sideways. How is that possible despite the solid dividend stream? The outlook for Omega looks good, as the number of people over 65 in the US is expected to grow by about 44% over the next 20 years. This will increase the demand for nursing home care. Omega itself says it is at the beginning of a "20+ year secular tailwind", mainly driven by the aging population, which is a strong catalyst.

A Risky Sector

However, the nursing home sector has faced headwinds in recent years. During the COVID-19 pandemic, nursing home occupancy rates plummeted while operators struggled with high costs. While occupancy rates are now well above 80%, many operators are operating on the verge of breakeven in terms of profit margins (the median operating profit margin in the sector is 0.6% in 2023!). Despite the demographic growth trend, the environment for operators has been challenging. Investors have likely been reluctant to invest, which has caused Omega’s share price to lag.

To reduce risk, Omega leases more than 900 healthcare properties to more than 30 different operators in the US and, more recently, the UK. Sector peer Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA) has about 400 facilities, and larger healthcare real estate companies like Ventas (VTR) and Welltower (WELL) have a more diversified portfolio including senior living and medical office space. This makes them less dependent on nursing homes alone. This diversification translates into a lower risk profile and therefore a lower dividend yield.

Despite the risk, Omega also sees growth opportunities. In April this year, it completed a major acquisition of 45 care homes in the UK and Jersey for $344 million. This portfolio earns Omega £25.9 million in annual rent, with a high initial cash yield of around 10%. The company continues to invest in the US, with acquisitions worth $112 million, including in Texas. These deals could further increase Omega’s rental streams and AFFO in the coming years.

Another growth catalyst is the recovery of financially challenged tenants. Some have struggled in recent years, leading operator LaVie Care Centers to file for Chapter 11 protection in June 2024. Omega provided $10 million in emergency credit, and LaVie has made all of its monthly rent payments to date. Rent has also increased slightly this year due to indexation.

Maplewood Senior Living is also showing clear progress, with occupancy up to 94% and a quarterly rent increase of $1.3 million compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.

In March, one of Omega’s largest tenants, Genesis Healthcare, unexpectedly missed its rent payment. Omega immediately stepped in to call in some of its collateral, but Genesis resumed payments in April. Genesis has a cash flow coverage ratio of 1.6x rent (according to Omega) and its credit position is collateralized.

I am not satisfied that a number of tenants are having rent payment problems; the low profit margins of the operators will be the cause of this. This seems to be a structural problem.

Furthermore, the developments are positive which indicate that tenants in need are becoming solvent again. Omega also has the possibility to transfer real estate to financially healthier operators if necessary.

A Lot Of Progress

Omega's financial performance is good. In the first quarter, revenues increased to $277 million and AFFO amounted to $221 million (or $0.75 per share versus $0.68 a year earlier). This increase is strong because Omega also issued additional shares to finance new real estate. The higher rental income more than compensated for this. With $0.75 AFFO and $0.71 FAD (funds available for distribution), dividend coverage is healthy, and also the balance sheet has been strengthened.

For fiscal year 2025, management has revised its profit forecast upwards and now expects an AFFO of approximately $3 per share. This forecast assumes that there will be no further setbacks. With the new outlook, the dividend payout ratio will drop to 90% of AFFO which provides more certainty or room for further dividend growth.

Furthermore, the balance sheet has been strengthened and approximately 7 million new shares have been issued, generating proceeds of $264 million. These proceeds, together with the available cash, have been used to repay debt and finance investments. As a result, leverage has been reduced to a net debt of only 3.7x EBITDA, the lowest debt level for Omega in more than 10 years. In addition, Omega still has $368 million in cash and a $1.45 billion credit facility that is still not used as a liquidity buffer. The balance sheet has thus been significantly strengthened and offers room for further growth by financing investments. However, a substantial amount of $600 million matures in January 2026 and Omega has indicated that it will fully repay this loan with its own cash and the issuance of shares. If this is not possible, interest rates will (expectedly) have fallen further, making refinancing more attractive.

High Dividend Yield, Attractive Valuation

REITs are particularly interesting because of their dividend. Omega Healthcare Investors now pays out $2.68 in dividends annually, which equates to a dividend yield of a nice 7.5%. Another plus is that the dividend has not been reduced despite the difficult circumstances, but unfortunately, it is not growing either.

The main reason is the high payout ratio because it leaves a small buffer to absorb setbacks. Dividend growth will only return if the AFFO grows structurally or if the risks decrease.

The valuation is attractive and in line with sector peer Sabra but clearly cheaper than other REITs in the sector. The higher valuation of Ventas and Welltower reflects their diversification and higher growth expectations.

Conclusion

Omega Healthcare Investors offers a high dividend yield with low to medium growth expectations. However, the long-term trend is positive due to the aging population and increasing demand for care. Omega has proven to be able to survive difficult years without cutting the dividend. Omega is also showing improvement because the AFFO is growing again, the balance sheet is strong, and the outlook is positive. At the same time, there are uncertainties in the short term because the margins of operators are very low, which makes the rental income uncertain. Therefore, high wage inflation could easily put further pressure on the margins. I give Omega a reiterated buy recommendation for the time being because the outlook is improving.

data.title: Super Micro: Now You Really Need To Ignore The Short-Term Noise
data.description:
Supermicro: Hurt By Hopper-Blackwell Transition

Mixed signals are starting to appear over the AI server horizon. While the hyperscalers haven’t disappointed on their recent CapEx outlook for the rest of the year, the same cannot be said for the AI hardware assemblers such as Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) ("Supermicro"). In my previous SMCI article, I presented my views about why the stock was still primed for a recovery, although I highlighted that uncertainties could also hobble the near term clarity, stymying a more robust valuation upgrade which saw the stellar run peaking out in early 2024.

While I believe acceptable downside has already been baked into its valuation as the FOMO was leveled off and normalized, Supermicro hasn’t endeared itself in encouraging more dip buyers to return with more conviction at its earnings conference yesterday. As a result, the relatively downbeat assessment that followed its preliminary update could face another round of digestion. The market will likely parse whether more critical headwinds could cause a second half growth inflection from occurring, although management has kept its confidence of overcoming the delays in the Blackwell architecture transition by the September calendar quarter.

First up, let’s visit the salient points from Supermicro's earnings release.

Supermicro's Worst Could Be Behind Us

I don’t think I need to alert you to the discernible 19% QoQ decline in its revenue trajectory, as the company faced challenges in augmenting its Blackwell ramp for FQ3. In addition, the inventory hurdles, which also saw the cash conversion cycle increased by 20 days to 124 days. However, these concerns aren’t totally surprising, as management had provided some snippets into the hitches when it updated us with the prelim release.

Moreover, it isn’t all doom and gloom for Supermicro. Why? Its cash from operations have improved to $827M, suggesting that the most pressing liquidity challenges might be over. While guidance obviously disappointed as it came in even below the lower end of its previous outlook range, we must also ascertain whether there’s enough confidence for the investors to contemplate a nadir in Supermicro’s operating performance.

Let me put it this way. When we parse earnings releases, I believe there’s bound to be hits and misses, and I surely am not expecting a perfect scorecard. Moreover, with SMCI’s valuation already normalized from the 2024 FOMO zenith, I don’t think investors are asking for the sky from management during this transition. Which plausibly explains why, while the stock is down almost 4% as I pen this update, we didn't witness this intense volatility that saw the stock fall to its lows at just above the $17 level.

In addition, there are several promising tailwinds that should continue to underpin the company's confidence in seeing a growth inflection in the second half of CY2025:

  • More clarity on the Blackwell ramp as hyperscalers aim to meet their committed CapEx footprint for the year.

  • More clarity on the trade negotiations over the next few months could afford more confidence for big tech to engage more earnestly on their spending plans.

  • Lowered recession forecasts should also help to provide more confidence in enterprise spending, helping to underpin the need for capacity growth for hyperscalers, which in turn also benefits Supermicro’s outlook.

  • SMCI remains among the first to market its Blackwell AI servers equipped with the latest DLC technology to meet even more stringent TCO and power efficiency requirements.

  • Nvidia (NVDA) remains confident in the long-term AI roadmap as articulated by Jensen Huang at a conference this week. Although I don’t expect him to say otherwise, his confidence about the broadening prospects of the AI thesis isn't misplaced.

  • AI leaders expect the monetization prospects to improve, with AI use cases potentially broadening across several industry verticals. In addition, enterprise adoption is still in the incipient stages, intimating that we should observe the growth profile to accelerate as the use cases get validated.

  • Also, Meta’s (META) decision to lift its midpoint CapEx outlook should provide more assurances to investors that big tech remains committed to realizing their long-term ambitions in AI.

I believe these pivotal observations underscore management’s commentary of a more robust Blackwell ramp in the second half, although the recent quarter’s disappointment also laid bare the challenges that could scupper its outlook if the execution falters.

SMCI's Valuation No Longer At FOMO Levels

Yet, Supermicro’s less than stellar outlook suggests that Wall Street estimates will undoubtedly need to be downgraded to reflect these challenges. With gross margins for FQ4 (10%) expected to be just slightly ahead of the 9.7% recorded for FQ3, it suggests that the worst outcomes should have been baked into the company’s fiscal third quarter release.

In other words, we should anticipate lower levels of inventory charges afflicting the previous Hopper generation as the company seeks to accelerate the inventory transition into the pivotal Blackwell ramp. Hence, if FQ3 was indeed the trough for SMCI during the current calendar year, the momentum imputed to Blackwell might be enough to lift the stock from its doldrums. Hence, I'm sanguine that more positive sentiments in the second half should underpin more confident momentum through the rest of 2025, as investors anticipate a bottom in its operating performance.

However, while the caution on Supermicro’s execution in the next two quarters will continue to be scrutinized, I must emphasize that SMCI’s valuation has normalized markedly. If we consider the crest that the stock struck in 2024 (well above the +2 standard deviation zone over its 10Y average), it’s now significantly below those levels.

Yet, given how volatile the stock has been, the bottom is assessed to be closer to the -1 standard deviation zone (4.7x forward EBITDA multiple). In other words, I believe the current posturing suggests the market doesn’t seem too bearish on the stock, aligning their expectations with management’s confidence that the forward outlook should yield improvements over Supermicro’s operating performance profile. Moreover, the battering that SMCI encountered as it fell to the valuation nadir was arguably attributed to the regulatory filings challenges, which have likely been resolved for now.

Management indicated that it’s on track to level up its domestic production capacity to afford it a distinct advantage over the overseas ODM peers who may struggle with their foreign capacities, taking into account the U.S. government’s commitment to re-shore the semi production value chain. Despite that, SMCI also highlighted the need for us to be more mindful of tariffs related headwinds that may not have been encompassed in the supposedly more cautious guidance as indicated in its 10% gross margin outlook.

Hence, there’s a reasonable downside possibility that further downgrades might be in store if the demand projections were to be less robust than what has been anticipated. With SMCI currently valued in line with its long-term average, the potential for further de-rating cannot be understated.

Notwithstanding the ambiguities discussed above, I believe that the secular AI growth impetus should still afford a much-needed boost to Supermicro’s bottoming prospects. SMCI’s price action has also been showing signs of improvement, as buying consolidation has also been heartening. Hence, I believe a Buy rating remains judicious, despite its valuation reflecting the near-term concerns over the uncertainties linked to the Blackwell ramp projections.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Gauging Qorvo's Financial Stance
data.description:
Qorvo (NASDAQ:QRVO) reported providing some interesting investment news that must be re-gauged. Management reported strong results in its non-cell phone markets and a major content increase in many, if not all, of the coming iPhones. The win came from products beforehand provided by Qualcomm (QCOM). Apple's (AAPL), thus far, successful commitment to exchange Qualcomm's modems for its own provides possible growth opportunities with many RF suppliers such as Qorvo, Skyworks (SWKS), or Broadcom (AVGO). Still, Qorvo's future isn't completely about Apple, but rather with its faster-growing businesses. Management spoke extremely positively about those opportunities, excepting tariff issues that might once again appear. This continues our coverage of the company, the last being, Qorvo's Business Turn Around: A Long And Winding Road. That long and winding road continues long and winding with viewing areas abounding. Shall we stop and view at one along the way and gauge progress.

Quarter

Let's begin with the quarter results summarized in bullet format.

  • Revenue of $869 million with non-GAAP earnings of $1.42.
    • Analysts' expectations equaled $850 million and $1.00.
  • Guidance, "quarterly revenue of approximately $775 million, plus or minus $25 million vs. $763.23M consensus."
    • June is the lowest quarter for Apple and lower than March, with its Android business.
  • Apple was 43% of the quarter.
    • For the December quarter, Apple represented 50% plus.
  • Revenue for the year: $3.7 billion.
  • FCF of $485 million for the year.
    • Purchased $350 million worth of stock.
  • Retired over $400 million of debt.
    • $1.4 billion in debt left and $1 billion of cash.

The stock price rose almost 15% the following day. Both the content news and the low price before the report likely contributed to the major lift. One analyst, Benchmark, raised its rating to a buy and lifted the price target to $95.

Business Review

For a reminder, the company divides itself into three segments, AGC (mobile), HPA (defense and aerospace plus), and CSG (Ultra-Wideband: positioning, Wi-Fi, etc.,). Key accomplishments include:

  • CSG and HPA grew revenue double digits.
    • Double-digit growth is expected to continue.
  • In CSG, the market funnel increased by a factor of four to $2 billion.
  • In AGC, they won an "envelope tracking Power Management solution."
    • Worth 10% content gain for its largest customer, Apple, beginning in the September quarter. (The 10% gain continues a long-term path.)
    • Qorvo continues its strategic investment with Apple, deepening growth prospects. (Apple actually plans and sets longer-term product development strategies.)
    • Samsung represented 10%.
  • Base station markets stabilized.
    • Early stages of DOCSIS 4.0 deployments, a technology in which Qorvo leads.
    • Growth begins.

They also included for the first time a laundry list of design wins within the mobile business including low-band, mid-high-band, and ultra-high-band paths, as well as mid-high secondary transmit, antenna tuning, discrete filters, and Wi-Fi 7 FEMs. The new ET win with Apple is custom and not expected to be ported elsewhere.

Adding to the report, analysts asked continually about pulling forward product ahead of tariff deadlines. Management answered,

"And, when I look at the overall business, with some things moving the quarter, some things move up the quarter, kind of normal. . .. I mean, we're not seeing anything massive moving one way or another right now."

Neither June guidance nor March results were impacted by product movements.

Margins for 2026 will trend higher, mainly from both product changes and the relocation of manufacturing sites. One product class, UWB, is moving to 22nm from 40nm.

Gauging a Path

In general, management supported its long-term business objectives. We begin with a look at FY-2026. With respect to HPA & CSG growing double-digit in 2026 was confirmed. They also reminded everyone that the China mid-range phone business is going away between $150 million to $200 million. With Samsung, additional content lost last year returns during the 2nd half of the year, increasing content from $1 to several dollars.

A smartwatch charging product begins shipping later in the year.

It appears to us that the Apple business gain replaces that loss. Qorvo generated approximately $3.7 billion, of which 50% came from Apple. The revenue math equals $3.7 times 0.5 times 0.1, or $185 million, approximately the same as the mid-range loss. It is important to remember that this starts in the September quarter, missing June. With double-digit growth elsewhere and an approximate revenue replacement from Apple, FY-2026 might grow slightly within a 0%–5% range.

Referring to 26, management also noted,

"We're repositioning the business for long-term success, so fiscal '26 is really more about execution and margin improvement to create diversification opportunities outside of mobile where we're on track.'

Now, let's look at some longer-term potentials. Srini Pajjuri of Raymond James asked for more detail on DNA (Defense). Philip Chesley, President of HPA, answered that the market is a $5 billion design win tunnel at this point, but the worldwide opportunity over the next five to ten years is $1 trillion, massive. Qorvo sees a path to a $1 billion annually within this business.

A major ASP content upgrade coming with Wi-Fi 7 in routers, access points, and extenders looms ahead. Development of Wi-Fi 8 started.

For autos, Qorvo started sampling of a fully integrated Ultra-Wideband programmable SOC. The uses are many. Finally, a design win with Japan for Wi-Fi 7 will begin shipping next year.

Management also included a general summary.

"Our growth and margin targets are anchored in a multi-year strategy focused on winning content with our largest customer, building on our core RF and power expertise to drive diversification through CSG and HPA, and continuing to operate our business and manufacturing footprint in a disciplined and efficient manner."

Charts

Now, let's head to the day chart.

We are using this chart pattern to determine option strike prices. From this chart, we are trying to sell strikes of approximately $90 in November.

Risk & Investing Rewards

The elephant in the room is related to tariffs and other macroeconomic conditions. Management left with this comment,

"Regarding our outlook for the current quarter, we are providing forward-looking quarterly guidance despite the continued uncertainty surrounding tariffs and broader macroeconomic conditions. Our view reflects numerous underlying assumptions, including those related to a dynamic global trade environment and ongoing supply chain challenges. For reference, in our June quarter guidance, we have assumed a direct tariff-related impact of less than $1 million."

Should the tariffs at the maximum level be reinstated, Qorvo noted that the price would be "high single-digit millions per quarter." The company seemed convinced that this would not happen. They also noted that they had found reasonable solutions in other periods where tariffs became impactful. We agree. In more recent news, Apple's influence on the administration on technology product tariffs with China seems impactful. Some of the news includes:

  • By 2027, Apple to import all iPhones sold in the US from India, rather than China.
    • Other news on this issue suggests that a larger portion of this year's iPhones will be produced, maybe as high as 30 million units.
    • India is working hard to create a fair-trade deal with the U.S.
  • Apple CEO Tim Cook reveals what Apple needs to build the iPhone in the US.
    • "According to Trump's Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, who was appearing on CNBC, Apple CEO Tim Cook told him that there is a "key catalyst" that would be necessary for Apple to consider building the iPhone in the US."
    • " Cook responded by saying, "I need to have the robotic arms to do it at a scale and precision that would allow me to bring it here. "Talking about the iPhone,
    • "Lutnick says about Tim Cook, "He wants to build it here, he's going to build it here."

Another interesting concept recently presented discussed Cook's long-term desire to move manufacturing from Asia to the United States, enabled through new, not yet fully developed robotic arm technology. Not yet developed high enough accuracy is still the missing function.

Another risk, which continues to pop up, is sharing functions with other suppliers, the two-supplier concept. Apple is smartly famous for this. Cirrus Logic (CRUS) is notably exempt for a multitude of reasons. But management explained that for years, it has shared business with others. With the new ET, they reminded investors that this was not possible any time soon because it is a very custom, highly integrated product with several years of design paths were needed. The shared risk with Apple is either already in place or not possible.

Now, let's review the reward. In our last article, we estimated the value of the future Qorvo. It includes this statement,

"After traveling the long and winding road, what can investors expect. Management stated:

  • Unchanged business targets, meaning:
    • A goal of HPA/CSG revenue greater than ACG.
  • FY-27: ACG growth of mid-single digits.
  • HPA & CSG: Long-term growth of double digits.
    • Guided the next two years at long-term double-digit growth."

Future results might equal this:

"An updated estimate can be summarized in the next revised table."

Management Model FY-25 FY-26 Target Percent Growth Approximate Revenue
AGC * $2.61 billion ** $2.5 billion *** <50% Mid-single (5%) $8.6 billion *****
CSG & HPA $1.12 billion $1.23 billion >50& Mid-Double (13%)

Management continued to signal that what is now a $3.5 billion revenue company with 45% margins. The vision suggests a company of $8.5 billion with 50% plus margins. This possibility turns into $3.0 billion–$3.5 billion in profits after tax. Qorvo spends approximately $1 billion in non-GAAP cash for R&D and SG&A per year. This value compares with $500 million in FCF during all of FY-2025. The change is close to an order of magnitude.

So, we are not sure that those operators checking the gauge have a big enough one to record the real potential. We will ask. The stock price in the $70s is rather cheap when compared with these possibilities. We also warn investors that news causing major volatility is highly likely going forward. We own a sizable portion and are hedging this investment without of the money short calls. News with reports of signed trade deals is likely to launch stocks, and news of tariffs could do the opposite.

Yet, we feel that the operators checking the gauge need to replace the too small one with a much larger one.

data.title: Franklin Small-Mid Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Markets: US equities experienced heightened volatility during the first quarter of 2025 amid concerns about President Donald Trump’s tariff policies and their impact on economic growth, inflation and the US Federal Reserve’s interest-rate path, with some investors fearing a potential recession or stagflation (economic stagnation and high inflation). Underwhelming earnings reports and guidance from some companies and significantly lower consumer confidence also hindered sentiment. US stocks collectively declined in the first quarter, with the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX), Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) and NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND) ending with losses. Small-capitalization stocks struggled over the quarter, followed by large- and mid-cap equities. In all three market-cap tiers, value stocks performed better than growth, with large-cap value stocks ending modestly positive.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the industrials sector was a leading driver of fund performance relative to the Russell Midcap Growth Index benchmark, followed by stock selection in the consumer discretionary, real estate and communication services sectors.
  • Detractors: Overall stock selection and allocation decisions worked against us and resulted in the fund’s underperformance versus the benchmark. Stock selection in the information technology sector had the biggest negative impact on relative returns; to a lesser extent, stock selection in the consumer staples and health care sectors also hindered relative performance.
  • Outlook: We expect the equity markets to remain volatile as tariffs and policy information unfold. For longer-term, patient investors, we think there are reasons for optimism. We believe that no matter what comes, innovators can find new ways to create value in a changing world. We are finding what we consider to be high-quality companies on sale today that we believe are resilient in tariff and recessionary environments and have excellent long-term prospects.

Performance Review

The fund underperformed the Russell Midcap Growth Index benchmark in the first quarter of 2025. In the IT sector, relative returns were hindered by a position in Manhattan Associates (MANH), which provides advanced supply-chain and omnichannel solutions to optimize inventory and logistics. The company reported fiscal-year 2024 earnings results that came in slightly above consensus expectations for revenue and profit margins. However, some investors reacted negatively to the company’s surprise guidance cut on 2025 services revenue. We believe the long-term prospects of its software business remain intact and warehouse software cloud migrations are a large growth opportunity.

In the consumer staples sector, pet food company Freshpet (FRPT) issued fourth-quarter 2024 financial results that missed consensus estimates, causing its stock to decline. We believe Freshpet has a superior product portfolio, offers a great consumer value proposition and enjoys several distinct competitive advantages. While the company reported a modest top-line growth deceleration, the quarter included strong volume trends and significant profit-margin upside following continued operational and cost-management improvements.

In contrast, our off-benchmark investment in DoorDash (DASH) was a leading contributor in the consumer discretionary sector. The company reported strong fourth-quarter 2024 revenue growth, with DashPass subscriber growth and repeat orders driving results. In our analysis, the company’s US business has been lucrative, and its international expansion is becoming profitable in certain markets.

Outlook

Recent equity market volatility is not surprising given the uncertainty about tariffs and their potential impact on the economy. As clarity emerges, we are likely to learn more about the new presidential administration’s investor-friendly policies, such as deregulation and tax cuts, which should help improve investor sentiment, in our view.

In broad terms, consumers continue to spend, employment is stable, corporate earnings remain fairly steady, and we are on the cusp of an innovation-led business cycle driven by artificial intelligence and factory reshoring (i.e., moving production back to the company’s home country). While the Magnificent Seven group of US mega-capitalization technology-related stocks played a key role in 2024’s equity market rally, we see growing opportunities beyond these market leaders.

As bottom-up fundamental investors, our focus is on analyzing individual companies rather than broader market trends or economic cycles. We invest in sectors that, in our analysis, deliver consistent, sustainable earnings growth and are backed by potentially sustainable secular growth tailwinds, such as IT with its advancements in AI. We are also paying attention to the industrials sector as the United States undergoes an industrial renaissance with companies reshoring their production and new technology making factories more competitive on a world-class basis.

Our strategy is designed to address both upward and downward market cycles, which are historically not unusual in equity markets. A diversified approach helps us participate in both ongoing market strength and anticipated rotation in market leadership. We invest in what we regard as great businesses positioned to potentially benefit from secular growth over a long-term horizon.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 12/31/1996. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (A) for Advisor Class periods prior to 12/31/1996, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 08/31/2025 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. Small- and mid- cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. Liquidity risk exists when securities or other investments become more difficult to sell, or are unable to be sold, at the price at which they have been valued. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Dow Jones Industrial Average is an unmanaged index composed of 30 blue-chip stocks, each with annual sales exceeding $7 billion. The DJIA is price-weighted, reflects large-cap companies representative of U.S. industry, and historically has moved in tandem with other major market indexes such as the S&P 500. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

The Magnificent Seven stocks are Apple, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla.

Nasdaq Composite Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to represent the performance of NASDAQ securities and includes over 3,000 stocks. Source: Nasdaq OMX. Stagflation is a seemingly contradictory condition described by slow economic growth and relatively high unemployment, or economic stagnation, which is at the same time accompanied by rising prices (i.e. inflation).

Stagnation is a prolonged period of little or no growth in an economy.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved. The Russell Midcap Growth Index measures the performance of the mid-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change. Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Not FDIC Insured | No Bank Guarantee | May Lose Value

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why The EU Construction Sector Is Steadily Outperforming The U.S.
data.description:
By Maurice van Sante

Without strategic initiatives aimed at boosting productivity and competitiveness, the EU risks falling behind its global rivals – at least, that's what the Draghi report tells us. This mainly applies to industries competing worldwide, like the manufacturing sector, and less so for the locally oriented construction sector. While the productivity development of the EU manufacturing sector lags behind its US counterparts, the construction sector in the EU outperforms that of the US. Yet, both regions have experienced a decline in labour productivity in construction. Still, a greater focus on productivity-enhancing measures could partly solve persisting labour shortages. For instance, we previously calculated that a 20% higher productivity rate in EU construction would result in 2.5 million fewer construction workers being needed.

Decline in construction labour productivity

Construction labour productivity lags considerably behind manufacturing. It has almost doubled in the US manufacturing sector over the past 25 years, meaning that manufacturing workers can now produce twice as much in the same number of hours. In the EU, this has risen by almost 60%. Growing efficiency means that the manufacturing industry produces more in each given working hour, often resulting in products becoming cheaper. Consider electronics, for example, where we saw prices fall for an extended period.

By contrast, construction labour productivity decreased by 25% in the US and by 15% in the EU in the same period. So, more construction workers are now needed for the same output.

EU construction labour productivity, far behind manufacturing, but ahead of US

Labour productivity of the added value in volume, index 2000=100

Source: Eurostat, Oxford Economics & ING Research

This raises several questions – all of which we aim to answer in this article.

  1. Why is a decline in labour productivity in construction problematic?
  2. Why is labour productivity lagging in the construction sector?
  3. Why are US contractors performing even worse than their EU counterparts?
  4. What can be done about it?

Why is a decline in labour productivity problematic?

Higher prices and labour shortages

Efficiency has dropped in the construction sector in both the US and the EU. Because manufacturing firms have made far greater efficiency gains than building contractors, the manufacturing sector has generated less inflationary pressure. Output prices also weren't raised as often due to efficiency gains.

Output prices across both regions have risen by roughly 35% over the last 25 years – but American construction prices have more than tripled over the same period. Clients of EU contractors were faced with smaller price increases, but still saw an uptick of almost 250%. Contracting is, in general, a local business; it doesn’t affect international competitiveness. However, it makes investments in new premises by firms or consumers more expensive. It therefore slows investments from companies and provides a headwind for new housing project development, while there are enormous shortages in many urban regions.

High price increases in US construction sector

Changes in output prices, index 2000=100

Source: Oxford Economics, Eurostat & ING Research

Low labour productivity growth not only affects prices but also causes construction staff shortages. By March 2024, more than 30% of EU building contractors could not complete all of their work because of personnel shortages. This can trigger knock-on effects, like undermining the huge task of making real estate more sustainable.

Nearly 14 million people work in construction in the EU. Additional productivity growth of 20%, for instance, could reduce the demand for additional construction workers in the EU by more than 2.5 million, as we mentioned previously. Productivity improvements may become even more important in the US, given immigration controls and the sector's high propensity to employ foreign workers. For the EU, the ageing workforce is also an important factor to consider.

Why does labour productivity lag behind in the construction sector?

Lagging productivity development isn't exclusive to the EU and the US, though; it's a phenomenon that ripples through the construction sector all over the world. In China and Japan, for instance, labour productivity growth in the construction sector is relatively low.

To pinpoint why exactly this is, we might look to the sector's culture, which is often described as traditional and averse to new ideas. It isn't that all construction firms are inherently opposed to innovation and increasing productivity, though. Construction's market structure plays a more important role here, and it's structurally different to that of the manufacturing sector. This comes down to a few key factors:

  • Production is tied to specific locations: Construction firms work at different locations for each job. Because production is tied to the location (i.e., the construction site), the construction process is more difficult to industrialise than if the work were to be undertaken in, say, a factory building. Heavy machinery is difficult to move, conditions differ at every location, and regulations vary from country to country. Flexibility is therefore highly important, and construction firms retain it by doing a lot of work manually. This also means that few construction firms operate in other countries, so foreign construction innovations are less readily implemented abroad.
  • Often building to someone else’s plan: Building designs are often created by architects (although this practice is dwindling) and then outsourced with specifications and drawings. Construction firms, therefore, have to build something new each time, and must comply with regulations and requirements that often differ from one municipality to the next. Imagine this happening in the automotive industry, with every buyer having their dream car built based on their design drawings. Industrialisation would hardly be possible in those circumstances. This also promotes "beginner’s mistakes" (failure costs), does not encourage industrialisation of the construction process, and results in scant investment in machinery (which can often perform only one type of task).
  • Volatile construction market: Construction firms must remain flexible as a result of the volatile demand for new construction. Investments in production resources drive up fixed costs. In times of crisis, this can prove ruinous. Because construction is so localised, it is almost impossible to spread risks internationally, with a resurgence in one market counterbalancing a crisis in another. Lastly, construction companies cannot produce stock, and cushion temporary shocks in demand by allowing stock to increase or decrease.

Need for flexibility makes innovation difficult

Source: ING Research

This isn't to say that the construction sector is hopelessly lost for innovation that improves efficiency, and we delve into how further progress can be made later on in this article.

Why are US contractors performing even worse than their counterparts in the EU?

As we noted earlier, the productivity decline in the US construction sector has been even more pronounced than in the EU. There are several reasons for this:

Sharper decline in output levels

A larger decline in construction production volumes is one of the main reasons that productivity in the US construction sector was even larger than in the EU. The US building industry did rebound after its fall during the financial crisis, but in 2024, construction output was still 8.4% lower than in 2000. The sector never fully recovered in the EU either, but the blow has been less pronounced; levels are now 4.8% lower than in 2000.

A drop in business turnover volume is usually not a fertile breeding ground for productivity. Contraction often creates overcapacity, meaning workers can be assigned less productively. These two elements also cause companies to invest less in new and more efficient machinery. Typically, there also aren't funds available for this, and because of the overcapacity, there's deemed to be no need for it. Economies of scale also diminish with contraction. In short, production levels being comparatively lower in the US than in the EU explains why labour productivity also remains lower.

Significant drop in US construction production

Volumes changes in construction production (added value, 2024 compared to 2000)

Source: Eurostat, Oxford Economics & ING Research

Lower investments in the US construction sector

To make construction workers more productive, they often need machinery, but US construction firms invest structurally less in this kind of capital. US contractors have invested on average 6% of their valued added since the beginning of this century, whereas EU contractors have nearly doubled that (11%). As mentioned above, a sharper decline in the US production levels could help to explain this – although investments were already lower in the US even before the financial crisis.

EU contractors invest more than US counterparts

Investments (capex) as a share of the value added

Source: Oxford Economics, ING Research

Fewer robotic machines on US construction sites

Lower investments in capital goods result in less robotic technology being used in the construction sector in the US compared to Europe. In the EU, 10,000 construction workers have 1.5 robots at their disposal; the US has just 0.60. For both countries, this is very low compared to the manufacturing sector, where there are more than 200 robots per 10,000 employees. In fact, it's so low that we'd say it probably has little real impact at all on productivity. Still, it proves useful in demonstrating once again how US contractors have fallen behind their EU counterparts when it comes to progress with new innovations and investments.

It's also interesting to see how, on average, EU contractors have more of these robotic machines than both China and Japan – although the number of robots is rapidly increasing in the Chinese construction sector.

EU contractors have many robots

Number of robots per 10,000 workers, 2023

Source: World Robotic, Oxford Economics & ING Research

Digitalisation goes slower in the US

Next to industrialisation, the digitalisation of construction processes is a good way to make efficiency gains. It can also significantly improve the information being provided during the building process. Managing the most up-to-date information flow to colleagues, subcontractors, and suppliers is all taken care of automatically, so all parties are directly informed of the latest adjustments. This also makes it far easier to prevent avoidable errors. Finally, there will only be one digital single source of truth (SSOT), which keeps everyone informed of the latest adjustments to the digital (3D) design and different steps in the building process. All parties are also made aware of real-time updates in the construction process.

Investing in digitalisation is yet another area in which European contractors have invested more than those in the US. The value of software per employee has almost doubled from 2000-2022 (from €546 to €1,065) in several European countries. In the US, it only increased by 44% (from $582 to $840) in the same period. These extra investments in digitalisation could have resulted in more productivity gains among EU contractors.

More digitalisation in European construction

Development software per employee in construction sector, 2000-2022

Source: OECD, Oxford Economics & ING Research

Composition effect of the subsectors

Some activities within the construction sector are even harder to industrialise than others. For instance, customisation is often needed (especially for renovation and maintenance activities), and this mainly falls into the specialised construction subsector. Infrastructure projects are usually more difficult to industrialise because they involve a great deal of customisation. As with specialised construction, digitalising can certainly help here.

Since the beginning of the 21st century, the share of these two subsectors has risen faster in the EU than in the US. This could have resulted in larger downward pressure on the total EU productivity development compared to the US – and, in turn, productivity development in the bloc's construction sector could actually be even better than the data above suggests.

Larger increased market share of civil engineering & specialised construction in the EU

Share of subsectors specialised construction & civil engineering of total construction output

Source: Oxford Economics, ING Research

What can be done?

Digitalisation, industrialisation and timber construction can increase productivity

Despite the obstacles we've covered, there are still ways for construction firms to increase their productivity, at least to some degree. Industrialisation – mainly involving machines and/or robotic machinery – could play a role here, for a more standardised way of working. Using timber could also help prompt more efficient construction; it's not only far more sustainable than other materials, but it's a good industrial product to work with due to the fact that it's much lighter, too. This means that large, prefabricated timber elements are easier to transport, can be processed with a greater degree of precision and are easier to attach. The lower weight also reduces the need for heavy machinery.

Another way to increase productivity is by digitalising the construction process, with digital tools helping to streamline projects and, in turn, make them more efficient. For example, providing information to all departments and chain partners can be greatly improved through digitalisation. Mistakes are also more likely to be avoided. Managing separate information flows to colleagues and subcontractors can be automated with digitalisation so that all parties involved are constantly and automatically informed of the latest adjustments (via SSOT).

More efficient construction is essential for every firm

Efficiency gains are key to coping with personnel shortages, ensuring that prices do not have to rise (too much) and keeping businesses competitive. Further digitalisation is essential in this regard. Barriers to digitalisation, such as initial investments and risks, are relatively limited. While industrialisation must certainly also be considered, it'd be wise to proceed with more caution here because of the initial high investments (plant, machinery and robots). Construction firms that fail to secure solid levels of efficiency will find themselves fishing in an increasingly small pond – and the shift towards a more sustainable and carbon-neutral business model will be made all the more difficult.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Novavax, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Novavax, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Adobe's Fundamentals Speak Louder Than Its Stock Price
data.description:
In this market that has been dominated by attention-grabbing headlines of AI disruption, earnings volatility and global uncertainty, Adobe Inc. (NASDAQ:ADBE) might be an intriguing contradiction. The share price has declined considerably over the past five months, but the business fundamentals remain strong. This disconnect offers us a unique opportunity, as long-term investors should be willing to look past the short-term noise to consider a business with enduring strength that may have been overlooked in today's hype driven stock market.

Many people will associate Adobe with its legacy software, Photoshop or Acrobat. However, the company has transformed and is based on a powerhouse of deep enterprise integrations and a continuously growing footprint of creative workflows enhanced by AI. Tools such as Adobe Firefly, which are now integrated into the Creative Cloud product, are not just flashy new features but represent entirely new monetization channels, deeper customer retention, and an overall strategic extension of Adobe's existing competitive moat. All of this, paired with resilient and consistent fundamentals, suggests that Adobe is not just surviving a disruptive marketplace; it is navigating and dominating its space with strategic clarity.

Profitability: In A Class of Its Own

We can see clearly that Adobe exhibits many of the hallmarks that value investors look for. These include very high and consistent profitability, excellent capital efficiency, and a durable competitive advantage. Looking at profitability, Adobe is among the best in the entire software industry. Boasting a gross margin of over 89%, this means that for every dollar in revenue, the company has about 90 cents left after accounting for the direct costs of providing its services. This is extraordinary and signals outstanding pricing power and operational leverage.

Operating margin stands at over 36%, and free cash flow (FCF) margin at almost 42%. We can see that Adobe converts a significant portion of its revenue directly into shareholder capital. It's important to note this is not recent either, as Adobe has done this consistently for many years.

Capital Efficiency and Making Every Dollar Count

If we look at capital efficiency, one of the most compelling numbers is the return on invested capital (ROIC) at 24%, which exceeds the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) at under 14%. This spread, which is often referred to as the value creation gap, tells us that Adobe is not just growing, but it's growing in a way that generates surplus value for shareholders in excess of the cost of capital. More simply, the business invests money at a high rate of return that is well above the cost of financing that growth, and this is one of the most important indicators of a competitive advantage in a business.

Looking at return on equity (ROE) and return on assets (ROA), respectively at around 47% and under 23%, these metrics reinforce not just profitability, but efficiency in Adobe's use of capital and assets to generate earnings. These numbers don't just talk about past success, but continue to point to a well-managed business that holds on to structural advantages that are highly likely to persist. This matters a lot because return metrics like these correlate strongly with long-term shareholder returns, especially when you pair them with disciplined capital allocation. Investors who focus on these types of businesses that repeatedly generate high returns on capital are taking advantage of the compounding engine that creates long-term wealth.

Discounted Cash Flow: The Value of Cash Flow, Not Hype

When looking at valuation, it's a combination of art and science. There is no perfect model, but the discounted cash flow (DCF) method remains one of the most useful tools for long-term investors trying to determine intrinsic value. This is where we examine a company's estimated fair value based on the present value of expected future cash flows. In Adobe's case, we arrive at a rough value of $593.49 per share, which is well above its recent market trading range of around $385 as of writing. The assumptions used play a big part in this calculation, and a big part of this is assuming that Adobe will maintain an earnings growth rate above 20% over the next 10 years. Some may not consider this a conservative estimate, but Adobe has a decent history of exceeding this number for at least the past 10 years. The implied margin of safety based on this is at least 35%, which gives investors a buffer if those growth rates prove difficult. In essence, Adobe does not need to beat the market or to dramatically outperform expectations to justify a higher valuation. It only needs to continue on the path it has been on for the past decade.

Financial Strength and Flexibility

When it comes to the balance sheet, we can see that this is just another layer of reassurance, particularly in a competitive and rapidly evolving landscape for Adobe. The company maintains pretty low levels of debt with a large amount of liquidity, which ensures it can get through any economic downturn or major shift in the industry without being forced into reactive cost-cutting or unfavourable financing.

This matters a lot because it gives Adobe resilience while it continues to fund research and development as customer demands evolve. Management should have more flexibility in looking at acquisitions as opportunities come up, and even look at share buybacks. Having this operational freedom gives Adobe even more of a competitive edge in looking to the future.

AI as an Accelerator, Not an Existential Threat

At the core of a decline in Adobe stock price is perhaps this overall assumption that free generative AI tools might replace Adobe's core offerings. On the contrary, I think this oversimplifies Adobe's value proposition and the position it maintains for its customer base. Many casual users might experiment with different tools, but professional users and, in particular, enterprise clients will continue to demand a high-quality, well-integrated, secure and compliant product suite that is very difficult to replicate and possibly impossible to find elsewhere.

We should also not overlook the major strides that Adobe has made in AI itself, which include Firefly's generative capabilities in text to image, generative fill and other enhancements that are part of the larger creative system. Acrobat AI assistant and GenStudio give users functionality to manage content at scale within secure and authentic platforms that these clients require.

An important note here is that Adobe's structured pricing seems to be well-received by customers, where AI tools are delivered on a tiered model. This allows a good entry point for casual users to access basic features, while more dedicated professional and enterprise clients can unlock all of the enhanced capabilities at higher tiers. This pricing model is part of a bigger average revenue per user strategy, reinforcing its wide economic moat. To put it simply, AI isn't replacing Adobe. It's making Adobe's platform more powerful than ever and catapulting value in the eyes of customers, who will increasingly see its products as the only comprehensive option for the tools they need.

The Market Disconnect

When we look at other enterprise software businesses, Adobe seems unusually cheap. Despite the metrics we have talked about, including gross margins, return on capital, etc. Adobe trades at a valuation that suggests investors are passing up this business on bigger fears. If we look at companies such as Salesforce, Oracle and Autodesk, which command higher forward multiples in the 20s, Adobe outpaces them on the fundamentals.

With a Forward PE of around 18.4, it is significantly undervalued compared to its five-year average, and this is also reflected in the price to free cash flow metric. The only central thesis that seems to prevail is that the market feels Adobe will face significant headwinds, whereas the reality seems to be a business with durable long-term growth and solid financial discipline.

Having discussed the quality of the business at length, we have also touched on several indications that tell us Adobe is undervalued, including the DCF analysis, as well as looking at price multiples versus competitors. The risks here are real, and there is a possibility that tools like Canva or even companies like OpenAI expand their product offerings to provide creative suites that degrade Adobe's value proposition in the eyes of customers.

The final takeaway is not that Adobe is a guaranteed winner, but rather that we see a high-quality business with durable advantages and consistent profit at a valuation that might underestimate its long-term value. In a market often distracted by noise, Adobe could be a good opportunity hiding in plain sight, whether you are building a long-term watch list or looking for a long-term holding.
